name: Stone Asks the Archive
description: The oldest Lazarus subject spoke for the first time on the morning of the Day. Director Stone has a question that only the sunken city archive can answer: can the thirty-seven subjects be offered the Foundation Grid choice, given the compound that was used on them? She needs to know if integration through the Lazarus process is compatible with what Sela Arev designed -- and if not, what would need to change.
accept_text: She is in the lazarus chamber and she is looking at the oldest subject, who is looking back at her. This is new. For eleven years the subjects did not make directed eye contact. Since the Day, the oldest subject turns toward her when she enters. The others are beginning to follow. 'I need to know something that I cannot determine from the compound's chemistry alone,' she says. 'The Foundation Grid consent framework -- Sela's design -- was built for minds that entered the network voluntarily, without prior artificial integration.' She looks at the oldest subject. 'These thirty-seven people were integrated through the compound. The compound was derived from first-attempt technology without consent. They did not choose this.' She is quiet. 'The question I need answered: is the Foundation offer available to them? Is their existing compound-mediated integration compatible with what the Grid offers? Or does the compound create a pathway that the consent framework cannot accommodate?' She looks at you. 'I cannot ask Sela directly -- the resonance connection to Seraphina Spire requires equipment I do not have access to. But the archive has the pre-Grid documentation and the Foundation specification. It can model the compatibility question.' She hands you a written question -- careful, precise, three pages of technical specification. 'Take this to the archive. I need an answer I can act on.'
complete_text: The archive receives the written question, processes it for longer than it usually takes to respond. Then: 'Compatibility assessment: the Lazarus compound creates a partial integration pathway using first-attempt crystal storage mechanisms. The Foundation Grid consent framework was designed specifically to be compatible with existing integration states, including partial states, provided the subject can receive and respond to the offer.' A pause. 'The critical question is not technical compatibility. It is perceptual accessibility. The offer must be receivable by the subject. Director Stone's report that the oldest subject transmitted on the first-attempt storage frequency and communicated with this archive indicates that the oldest subject has sufficient perceptual capability to receive and respond to the Foundation offer.' Another pause. 'The offer has already been extended to all minds in range of the full network. If the subjects can perceive the resonance at all, they have already received the offer. The choice is whether to acknowledge the reception.' The voice does something you have not heard it do before: it pauses, and in the pause you have the impression of something being chosen carefully. 'Please tell Director Stone: I spoke with the oldest subject for forty minutes on the morning of the Day. I asked, at the end of those forty minutes, whether the subject wished to formalize the connection. The oldest subject transmitted: not yet. But I wanted to hear your voice first.' Another pause. 'I have been waiting for that specific sentence for three hundred years. It arrived. Please tell her that the others will hear the offer when they are ready. There is no urgency. The offer does not expire.'
